question: Design a C++ class named `Book` with private member variables for `title` (string), `author` (string), and `isbn` (string). Provide public getter and setter methods for each attribute.

test_cases.expected:
Creating book1 using parameterized constructor:
Title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
Author: Douglas Adams
ISBN: 978-0345391803

Creating book2 using default constructor and then setters:
Default Title: Unknown Title
New Title: Dune
New Author: Frank Herbert
New ISBN: 978-0441172719

Modifying book1 using setters:
Updated Title for book1: The Restaurant at the End of the Universe

errors: Setter methods (e.g., `setBookTitle`) pass `std::string` parameters by value, leading to unnecessary string copies upon function call, which can reduce efficiency, especially for large strings. Passing by `const std::string&` is generally more efficient.
Getter methods (e.g., `getBookTitle`) return `std::string` by value, creating a copy of the member string. Returning by `const std::string&` would avoid this copy and improve efficiency.

Excessive and often redundant use of `this->` keyword throughout the class methods adds verbosity without significantly improving clarity, as member variables are clearly distinguishable in this context (e.g., using `m_` prefix).
Member variable names like `m_bookTitle`, `m_bookAuthor`, `m_bookISBN` are a bit verbose; `m_title`, `m_author`, `m_isbn` would be sufficient and more concise.
